did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning (CNNs: AlexNet, GoogLeNet, VGG16) for feature extraction and hybrid models combining CNNs with Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Random Forest (RF) for the classification and early detection of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dermoscopy dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes and evaluates new hybrid deep learning models (CNN-ANN and CNN-RF) for the early detection of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It presents original experimental results, including specific performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, specificity) for various model configurations, and details the methodology involving image segmentation (GAC), feature extraction (CNNs), and classification. The structure includes Introduction, Methods, Experimental Results, and Conclusion, which is characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: AI Techniques of Dermoscopy Image Analysis for the Early Detection of Skin Lesions Based on Combined CNN Features
brief_summary: This study proposes hybrid deep learning models combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Random Forest (RF) for the early detection and classification of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dermoscopy dataset. The methodology involves segmenting lesion areas via the Geometric Active Contour (GAC) algorithm, extracting features using AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and VGG16, and reducing dimensionality with Principal Component Analysis (PCA). The models were evaluated on their ability to distinguish melanoma and other skin cancers from benign lesions, with performance measured by AUC, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, and specificity. The AlexNet-GoogLeNet-VGG16-ANN hybrid model achieved the highest performance, yielding an AUC of 94.41%, accuracy of 96.10%, and specificity of 99.44%. These results demonstrate that fused CNN features combined with ANN or RF classifiers effectively diagnose dermatoscopic images and differentiate skin cancer from other lesions.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Squamous cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The models (CNN-ANN and CNN-RF hybrids) predict discrete lesion classes. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and AUC. Confusion matrices are provided to show class-wise accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopy image dataset for skin lesion diagnosis and prediction, composed of images from the HAM10000 and BCN_20000 datasets. It contains high-resolution RGB images with metadata including lesion location, sex, and age.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN-ANN and CNN-RF models. The training subset (80% of the dataset) was subjected to data augmentation to balance class distribution.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training phase (20% of the training subset, resulting in 20% of the total dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the proposed models. The testing subset comprised 20% of the total dataset.
use.other: The pre-trained CNN models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, VGG16) were originally trained on the ImageNet dataset, but the ISIC 2019 dataset was used for feature extraction and subsequent classification tasks in this study.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale image dataset used for pre-training the CNN models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, VGG16) before applying them to the dermoscopy images.

use.training: Used to pre-train the CNN models to gain experience and transfer features for the new dermatoscopic image classification tasks.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Dimensionality reduction of high-depth feature maps extracted by CNN models before classification.
Evaluation of classification performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, specificity) for individual lesion classes.
Aggregating performance results (AUC, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, specificity) across classes to compare hybrid models (CNN-ANN and CNN-RF).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as AUC, sensitivity, accuracy, precision, and specificity.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid models combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Random Forest (RF). Specifically: CNN-ANN and CNN-RF hybrids. Individual CNN backbones used: AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and VGG16. Fused feature models: AlexNet-GoogLeNet-ANN, GoogLeNet-VGG16-ANN, AlexNet-VGG16-ANN, AlexNet-GoogLeNet-VGG16-ANN, and corresponding RF variants.
architecture_used.pretraining: AlexNet, GoogLeNet, and VGG16 were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (over 1,200,000 images, 1000+ classes).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported as pixel dimensions. Input was dermoscopy images from the ISIC 2019 dataset, specifically the Region of Interest (ROI) segmented by the Geometric Active Contour (GAC) algorithm.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: CNN models (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, VGG16) processed ROI images to produce high-depth feature maps. Feature matrix sizes before PCA: 25,331 x 4096 for all three models. Feature matrix sizes after PCA: 25,331 x 610 (AlexNet), 25,331 x 590 (GoogLeNet), 25,331 x 640 (VGG16).
architecture_details.fusion_method: Serial integration of feature vectors from multiple CNNs. Fused feature matrix sizes: 25,331 x 1200 (AlexNet-GoogLeNet), 25,331 x 1230 (GoogLeNet-VGG16), 25,331 x 1250 (AlexNet-VGG16), 25,331 x 1840 (AlexNet-GoogLeNet-VGG16).
architecture_details.dimensionality_reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied to reduce high-dimensional feature maps and remove redundant features.
architecture_details.segmentation: Geometric Active Contour (GAC) algorithm used to segment lesion areas from healthy skin.

training_details.dataset_split: ISIC 2019 dataset split into 80% training/validation (80:20 ratio) and 20% testing. Training subset subjected to data augmentation to balance class distribution.

classification_or_output_head.classifier_type: Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Random Forest (RF).
classification_or_output_head.details: Representative feature matrices (post-PCA or post-fusion) were fed into ANN and RF networks for classification. Specific ANN architecture (layers, neurons) and RF hyperparameters (trees, depth) were not reported.

preprocessing_techniques: 6x6 averaging filter (2D convolution operator) for noise and artifact removal
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for contrast improvement
Geometric Active Contour (GAC) algorithm for lesion segmentation and Region of Interest (ROI) extraction
Data augmentation to balance class distribution in the training subset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance class distribution in the training subset. Specific operations included rotating images at various angles, resizing, vertical and horizontal transformations, vertical and horizontal flipping, displacement, and shearing.

Images were increased by specific multipliers per class to achieve balance: Squamous cell carcinoma (Scc) increased by 20 times, Actinic keratoses (Akiec) by 15 times, Basal cell carcinoma (Bcc) by 4 times, Benign keratosis lesions (Bkl) by 5 times, Dermatofibroma (Df) by 40 times, Melanoma (Mel) by 3 times, and Vascular (Vasc) by 40 times. The Melanocytic nevi (Nv) class was not augmented as it contained sufficient images.

conclusions: Hybrid models based on fused CNN features achieved promising results for diagnosing dermatoscopic images of the ISIC 2019 dataset and distinguishing skin cancer from other skin lesions.
The AlexNet-GoogLeNet-VGG16-ANN hybrid model achieved the highest performance with an AUC of 94.41%, sensitivity of 88.90%, accuracy of 96.10%, precision of 88.69%, and specificity of 99.44%.
Among single CNN-ANN hybrids, the AlexNet-ANN model achieved an AUC of 89.36%, sensitivity of 89.36%, accuracy of 94.80%, precision of 89%, and specificity of 99.14%.
Among single CNN-RF hybrids, the AlexNet-RF model achieved an AUC of 90.30%, sensitivity of 86.69%, accuracy of 94.30%, precision of 85.45%, and specificity of 97.76%.
The AlexNet-VGG16-ANN hybrid model based on fused features achieved an AUC of 94.38%, sensitivity of 88.54%, accuracy of 95.2%, precision of 89.96%, and specificity of 99.23%.
The AlexNet-GoogLeNet-ANN hybrid model based on fused features achieved an AUC of 90.83%, sensitivity of 84.8%, accuracy of 95%, precision of 88.49%, and specificity of 98.89%.
The GoogLeNet-VGG16-ANN hybrid model based on fused features achieved an AUC of 90.68%, sensitivity of 86.96%, accuracy of 94.6%, precision of 86.86%, and specificity of 87.83%.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which has a highly imbalanced class distribution (e.g., Melanocytic nevi has 8,240 training images vs. Dermatofibroma with 153), necessitating aggressive class-specific data augmentation (up to 40x for rare classes) to balance the training set.

The CNN backbones (AlexNet, GoogLeNet, VGG16) were pre-trained on ImageNet, a general object recognition dataset, which does not contain biomedical or dermatoscopic images, potentially limiting the relevance of transferred features.

Critical hyperparameters for the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Random Forest (RF) classifiers, as well as the CNN training details (optimizer, loss function, learning rate, epochs), are not reported in the paper.
The specific architecture of the ANN classifier (number of layers, neurons per layer) and the hyperparameters of the Random Forest (number of trees, max depth) are not disclosed.
The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to validate the significance of the performance differences between models.

External validation was not performed; the models were evaluated solely on the held-out 20% test split of the ISIC 2019 dataset, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The segmentation method uses the Geometric Active Contour (GAC) algorithm, but the paper does not provide a ground truth comparison or error analysis for the segmentation step, which is a critical upstream component.

Data leakage risk exists if the data augmentation was applied before the train/validation/test split, although the text states augmentation was applied to the training subset; however, the exact split timing relative to augmentation is not explicitly detailed in the methodology section provided.
